description: When either path leads to the same city, the fork doesn't matter.

guides.text: The goal is $(A \lor B) \to (B \lor A)$. If you squint, it just says: '$A$ or $B$' and '$B$ or $A$' are the same claim. [disjunction|Disjunction] doesn't care about order. But we have to **prove** it, which means we need a new tool: proof by cases.
Start as usual for an [implication|implication]: assume the left side, $A \lor B$. This will be the [disjunction|disjunction] we eliminate by cases.

The strategy: we'll prove $B \lor A$ **twice** — once from assuming $A$, once from assuming $B$. If both paths reach the same destination, then the [disjunction|disjunction] $A \lor B$ guarantees that destination. First, assume the left disjunct: $A$.
From $A$, we need to build $B \lor A$. Since $A$ is our truth and we want it on the **right** of $\lor$, use `or-intro-r`. The left side can be anything — let it be $B$.
One case done. Now assume the right disjunct: $B$.
From $B$, we again need $B \lor A$. This time $B$ goes on the **left**, so use `or-intro-l` with $A$ as the right side.

Here is the payoff. We have $A \lor B$ at step 1. From $A$ we proved $B \lor A$ (step 3). From $B$ we also proved $B \lor A$ (step 5). The rule `or-elim` — **proof by cases** — lets us conclude $B \lor A$ outright. It takes three arguments: the [disjunction|disjunction], the left-case proof, and the right-case proof.
We've reached $B \lor A$ under the [assumption|assumption] $A \lor B$. One final `->-intro` discharges that assumption and the proof is complete.

unlocks.name: ∨-elim
unlocks.rule: \frac{\Gamma \vdash p \lor q \quad \Delta_1, p \vdash r \quad \Delta_2, q \vdash r}{\Gamma \cup \Delta_1 \cup \Delta_2 \vdash r}
unlocks.description: If $p \lor q$ is true, and both $p$ and $q$ independently lead to $r$, then $r$ is true regardless. Reason by cases — exhaust the possibilities.
